exemplar.title: To what extent does social media create symptoms of depression in adolescents through social comparison, cyberbullying, and excessive time spent online?

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent does social media create symptoms of depression in adolescents through social comparison, cyberbullying, and excessive time spent online?

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

strengths: Introduction situates topic clearly with rationale linking smartphone adoption to adolescent mental health trends
Section headings and subtopics align well with the research question, providing coherent structure
Title communicates focus effectively and the purpose remains apparent throughout the essay
weaknesses: Research question wording is inconsistent and convoluted across the text
No explicit methodology section outlining search strategy or inclusion criteria
Minor grammatical errors and formatting lapses (e.g., broken table of contents) detract slightly from clarity
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Given the consistent communication of purpose and focus, coherent structure, and clear linkages to the research question, the submission demonstrates strong performance in Focus and Method. However, inconsistency in the research question phrasing and the absence of an explicit methodology section prevent a topâband score, placing it at the upper end of band 3â4, warranting a final score of 5.

strengths: Accurate explanation of major depressive disorder using DSM-5 criteria
Effective application of Festingerâs social comparison theory to frame analysis
Use of authoritative sources (e.g., UNICEF, DSM-5) demonstrates sound understanding
weaknesses: Occasional factual slips and overly broad language (e.g., âprovenâ rather than âreportedâ)
Reliance on some less rigorous web sources rather than primary definitions
Uneven source quality limits excellence in knowledge application
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student shows good subject knowledge and generally accurate use of terminology and concepts, anchored by sound explanations and integration of theory. Occasional factual imprecision and uneven source quality, however, prevent this from reaching the excellent level, placing it firmly in band 3â4 and meriting a score of 4.

strengths: Wide range of empirical studies drawn from reputable journals and organisations, demonstrating comprehensive research
Critical evaluation of study limitations (e.g., demand characteristics, self-report bias) shows analytical depth
Balanced argument weighing both negative and positive effects of social media, culminating in a coherent synthesis
weaknesses: Synthesis across studies can be patchy, with some evaluative points remaining surface-level
Overstatement of causality despite cross-sectional data; clearer correlation language needed
Statistical detail (e.g., effect sizes, fit indices) is often missing, limiting depth
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission exhibits solid critical thinking: research is relevant and wellâchosen, analysis is clear and generally well supported, and the argument is reasoned with critical evaluation. While synthesis and statistical detail could be deeper and causal language more accurate, overall performance sits at the top of band 4â6, justifying a score of 6.

strengths: Basic academic structure is present (introduction, themed sections, discussion, conclusion)
Title, word count, and APA reference list are correctly provided
weaknesses: Table of contents is incomplete and formatting is broken
Section numbering is inconsistent and no page numbers are shown
Layout inconsistencies (placeholder lines, LaTeX remnants) detract from readability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The report meets some layout and structural conventions but has several omissionsâbroken table of contents, inconsistent heading styles, and missing page numbersâthat place it in band 1â2 for Presentation. The performance is solidly âgenerally appropriateâ but not yet âclearly appropriate,â supporting a score of 2.

strandSelections.reasoning: Key psychological termsâmajor depressive disorder, anhedonia, social comparison, FOMO, demand characteristicsâare used largely correctly and repeatedly.  There are sporadic inaccuracies and grammar issues, but terminology is mostly accurate and concepts are integrated into the argument, aligning with the 3-4 descriptor.

The student supplies a title, word count and APA-style reference list, and headings are clearly distinguished.  Nevertheless, formatting is uneven (placeholder lines, LaTeX code remnants, no visible page numbers), so only âsome layout considerationsâ are correct.

The student summarises and applies a range of psychological studies (Kelly et al., Coyne et al., Sampasa-Kanyinga & Hamilton, Nesi & Prinstein) and refers to diagnostic criteria (DSM-5) and measurement instruments (CES-DC, K-10). Explanations of findings and limitations show sound understanding.  Occasional factual slips and uneven source quality (popular websites alongside journals) keep the work at âgoodâ rather than âexcellentâ knowledge and application.

The essay adopts a literature-review methodology and uses multiple peer-reviewed studies, national surveys, and WHO statistics.  The student comments on sampling, measures, ethics and validity for each study, demonstrating informed selection.  However, there is no explicit methodological section outlining search strategy, inclusion criteria, or justification of scope, and some sources (e.g. web pages) are less academic.  Hence methodology is âmostly completeâ with evidence of thought, but not yet âcomplete and appropriateâ.

The student states the investigationâs focus up-front (âAn investigation into Social Media and Depression in Adolescentsâ) and immediately situates the issue in current psychological debate, explaining why the topic matters (rise in teen mental-health problems since 2012). The introduction and section headings make the purpose (to examine whether social-media use leads to depressive symptoms) clear for the reader.  Minor lapses in clarity (e.g. broken table-of-contents formatting, some grammatical miscues) prevent a fully polished communication, but overall the topic, purpose and focus remain consistently apparent throughout the essay.

The bibliography and body cite over a dozen empirical studies drawn from reputable journals and international organisations, covering several mechanisms (cyberbullying, comparison, time use).  While some website sources are less rigorous, the majority are relevant and well-chosen for addressing the research question, indicating solid (though not outstandingly comprehensive) research quality.

The research question is explicitly presented twice, but wording is awkward and slightly inconsistent (âTo what extent does social media create symptoms of depression in through social comparison, adolescents cyberbullyingâ¦â versus ââ¦cause depression in teenagersâ).  Despite the phrasing issues, the question is sufficiently narrow (social-media use â depressive symptoms in adolescents) and the later structure of the paper connects clearly to it.  Because of the imprecision and duplication, the focus is only partially sharp rather than fully refined, matching the 3-4 band.

Basic academic structure (introduction, themed sections, discussion, conclusion, references) is present, but the table of contents is incomplete, section numbering inconsistent, and some headings appear inside a broken table.  These omissions show a âgenerally appropriateâ rather than fully appropriate structure.

The essay advances a reasoned argumentâsocial media contributes to depression primarily via mediatorsâand weighs counter-evidence (positive effects).  Limitations, ethics and future research are discussed.  However, the argumentâs structure is occasionally disjointed, and evaluation sometimes lists rather than critiques.  Thus the strand fits solidly within 4-6, at 5 marks.

For each study the student analyses design, sample size, validity and limitations, then ties findings back to the overall question.  The analysis moves beyond description, yet synthesis across studies is patchy and some evaluative points are surface-level or repetitive (e.g. repeated mention of self-report bias).  This places the work mid-band (4-6), best represented by 5.

annotations.comment: Opening paragraph cites multiple findings as âprovenâ; academic writing requires cautious language (e.g., âstudies report increased ratesâ).
The âBodyâ heading is present, but section numbering is inconsistent; align numbering with the ToC and use consistent heading styles.
Inline LaTeX code (e.g., <math display="inline">\&</math>) disrupts readability; remove or convert to plain text as per APA style.
The introduction clearly communicates research focus and rationale, linking to smartphone adoption in 2012 which grounds the investigation.
Statement of focus and subheadings for psychosocial mechanisms aligns well with the research question and provides clear structure.
The title succinctly communicates the investigationâs focus and situates it clearly in the context of adolescent mental health research.
Wording of the research question is inconsistent and convoluted; refine it to a single clear formulation (e.g., âTo what extent does social media use cause depressive symptoms in adolescents?â).
Providing the word count at the start meets IB formatting requirements; ensure this remains accurate if revisions alter length.
Table of contents is incomplete and lacks page numbers; populate all entries and fix broken formatting to guide the reader.
Placeholder LaTeX code appears in the table; remove or replace with descriptive headings to avoid confusion.
Definition of social media relies on a secondary website; strengthen subject understanding by using primary WHO or original source definitions.
Explanation of major depressive disorder anchored in DSM-5 criteria is accurate and demonstrates sound subject knowledge.
There is no explicit methodology section outlining search strategy or inclusion criteria; add this to demonstrate informed source selection.
Discussion of the Millennium Cohort Study is detailed; however, clarify why this cohort was prioritized over others to strengthen justification.
Sample description includes key demographics and sweep intervals, demonstrating good understanding of longitudinal design.
Causal language is used despite cross-sectional data; revise to reflect correlation and avoid overstating causality.
Mention of demand characteristics and self-report bias demonstrates critical evaluation of study limitations.
Later discussion lumps different negative mediators together; consider separate sub-sections for cyberbullying, social comparison, and time use for clarity.
Coyne et al. study sample is regionally constrained; discuss implications for cross-cultural validity and generalizability.
Growth mixture modeling is applied well, but effect sizes and model fit indices (e.g., $$AIC$$, $$BIC$$) should be reported to strengthen analysis.
The discussion of normative moderate use is insightful but could tie back more explicitly to the research question.
Definition of cyberbullying is clear and supported by UNICEF citation, demonstrating good use of authoritative sources.
Reflection on methodological weaknesses (e.g., self-report) shows analytical engagement; consider proposing observational measures.
Discussion section cogently addresses ethics and demand characteristics; however, restructuring into sub-topics would improve clarity.
Citations lack uniform DOI presentation and date access format; ensure each reference follows APA guidelines precisely.
Final page contains an unrelated LaTeX formula; remove this remnant to maintain professionalism.
Mediation analysis is appropriate, but statistical details (e.g., mediation effect size, confidence intervals) are missing; include these metrics.
Non-response rate (37%) is substantial; discuss potential bias from parental refusal and its effect on validity.
Positing that cyberbullying mediates SM and depression is insightful, but could be strengthened by integrating empirical path coefficients.
Use of Festingerâs social comparison theory provides solid conceptual grounding for the next sectionâs analysis.
Sample of only 0.5% Asian participants is small; discuss limitations for subgroup analyses and cultural applicability.
Balancing negative with positive effects of social media shows critical evaluation and addresses potential biases.
Conclusion synthesizes findings well, emphasizing the non-linear relationship and mediator roles coherently.
Future research suggestions are broad; specify exact designs (e.g., longitudinal mixed-methods with digital trace data) to make them actionable.
References list shows inconsistent APA formatting (e.g., missing italics, hanging indents); apply a uniform style throughout.
